title: Serpentwithfeet and the Gospel of Black Queer Tenderness
author: Allastair Voss
description: Josiah Wise, who records as Serpentwithfeet, grew up singing in evangelical Black church in Baltimore. The church vocal tradition gave him a voice trained
image_alt: Serpentwithfeet and the Gospel of Black Queer Tenderness
body:
## A Voice That Does Not Apologize

Josiah Wise, who records as Serpentwithfeet, grew up singing in evangelical Black church in Baltimore. The church vocal tradition gave him a voice trained in a specific emotional grammar: call and response, melisma, the extended note held until it becomes something else, the moment in a gospel phrase where technique and feeling become impossible to separate.

[spotify:artist/4bN93tOVACGYKqHSuB3K7V]

His music carries all of this into a context the church would not recognize. The love he sings about is the love between Black men. The tenderness he describes is specific to his experience of Black queer intimacy. There is no hedge in any of it. The devotional intensity that gospel cultivates for theological purposes, Serpentwithfeet directs toward the bodies and hearts of his partners.

This is not provocation. It is documentation. The distinction matters because provocation positions the audience as the intended recipient, performs transgression for effect, courts discomfort as a goal. Documentation does not perform for an audience. It records what is already there. Wise is not asking permission. He is not staging a confrontation. He is simply describing what he sees and feels with the fullest vocabulary available to him, which happens to include one of the most emotionally charged musical traditions in American history.

## Soil and the First Statement

The 2018 EP Soil arrived on Tri Angle Records, a label known for its ambient and experimental releases, and sat oddly and perfectly in that context. The production was abstract, at times barely there, more space than sound. Against it, Wise's voice was unapologetically operatic and gospel-trained and enormous.

Soil was not trying to find crossover appeal. It was not interested in presenting its subjects in ways that made listeners comfortable. The arrangements created a kind of beauty that could hold discomfort, the way late Romantic lieder can hold grief, the way some spiritual music creates space for doubt rather than resolution.

The label partner and producer Haxan Cloak understood that the voice needed room rather than frame, and gave it a production environment that felt like a field rather than a box. That decision had real consequences for how the music landed. A more conventional R&B or gospel production would have provided familiar scaffolding, would have helped listeners orient themselves. Removing that scaffolding forced the voice to carry everything, which is precisely where Wise is strongest. The sparseness made the emotional directness more confrontational, not because the music was aggressive, but because there was nowhere to look but at the voice and what it was saying.

## Deacon and the Turned Frequency

The 2021 album Deacon is a different proposition. The production is warmer, rooted in R&B and gospel arrangement more directly, with strings and piano and a clarity of emotional intention that Soil held at more of an angle.

What does not change is the specificity of the love being described. Deacon is an album about Black gay love as an ordinary fact of life. Not a political statement, not a corrective to representation, just: this is what love looks like, sounds like, feels like from here.

The title track and the single "Fellowship" operate in that register. The music is generous, full of space and warmth, and the relationship it describes is one where tenderness is not a vulnerability but a language. The church metaphors are real and structural: Deacon is the role of service, of support, of holding a community together from the inside.

Wise applies that metaphor to intimate partnership. The beloved is the community. The devotion is the practice. That framing elevates the material without making it abstract. Love as service, as presence, as the daily work of keeping something alive, is a more concrete and demanding proposition than love as feeling. Deacon is interested in love as practice.

The album also benefits from the production choices in ways Soil did not quite achieve. Where Soil's abstraction created distance as a side effect of its ambition, Deacon's warmth feels earned. The strings are not decorative. The piano is not filling space. Each arrangement choice corresponds to an emotional shift in the song it supports. The craftsmanship is more visible and it is worth looking at.

## What the Voice Carries

The critical vocabulary for describing Serpentwithfeet's voice tends toward the operatic, and that is accurate as far as it goes. The range is wide. The phrasing draws from trained technique. The control is exceptional.

But the comparison that gets closer is gospel: specifically the Black gospel tradition where the voice is understood as an instrument for conveying something that exceeds the voice's technical capacity, where what matters is not the control but what the control is in service of.

In that tradition, to sing is to mean it in a way that requires every technique you have and still reaches beyond it. The voice cracks because what it is carrying is too large. The melisma extends because the emotion does not finish at the end of the note.

Serpentwithfeet applies this understanding to secular love and produces something that functions similarly: music that suggests the experiences it describes are as serious as anything the church ever processed, as worthy of that full intensity of feeling. The transfer of technique is also a transfer of gravity. To sing about a man's hands with the same vocal investment that a gospel singer brings to the name of God is to make an argument about importance. It is an argument Wise never makes explicitly and never has to.

## GRIP and the Evolution

The 2023 album GRIP moved further into R&B, with a cleaner production aesthetic and collaborations that emphasized the pop dimensions of the voice. The reception was divided in the way that mainstreaming always divides: some listeners felt the abstraction and challenge had been sanded down, others heard a fully realized version of the sound.

What remained was the voice and the commitment. The love being documented did not change registers. The tenderness did not become ironic or apologetic or coded. GRIP is the record where Serpentwithfeet sounds most comfortable in his own skin, and comfort is not the same as complacency. The production is polished but not sanitized. The emotional range is narrower than Deacon in some respects, but the execution within that narrower range is tighter.

Across all three records, Serpentwithfeet has made essentially the same argument: that Black queer love deserves the same gravity of treatment that any tradition of devotional music has given to its objects of devotion. The gospel-trained voice is the medium because the message is, in its way, gospel: here is what love looks like, it is specific and it is real and it asks nothing of you except attention.

## Why the Work Holds

The reason Serpentwithfeet's catalog holds together as a body of work, rather than as three distinct sonic experiments, is that the emotional and political project never wavers. The production changes. The collaborators change. The level of accessibility to a mainstream R&B audience changes. What does not change is the refusal to code-switch, the insistence that Black queer intimacy does not need to justify or explain itself to be worth documenting with the fullest possible musical resources.

That consistency is rarer than it appears. Many artists make one uncompromising record and spend the rest of their career managing the fallout or retreating toward the center. Wise has continued to work in the same territory with increasing confidence and craft. That is not a small thing. It is the kind of artistic commitment that takes years to recognize for what it is.
